questions.question: Does `grep -r "onApplicationMessage" app/src/ --include="*.ts" --include="*.tsx"` return zero matches after Story 4 ships — confirming the symbol has been completely removed from `welcomeSubscription.ts`, `MarmotContext.tsx`, and all other files in `app/src/`?

Does the `EpochResolverCallbacks` type (or equivalent options interface) in `app/src/lib/marmot/welcomeSubscription.ts` contain only `onHistorySynced` and `onMembersChanged` fields after the removal — with no `onApplicationMessage` field present anywhere in the type definition or any related interface in that file?

Could `welcomeSubscription` be deleted and rewritten without modifying any file outside its `dependencies_allowed` list? Specifically: does removing `onApplicationMessage` from the `subscribeToGroupMessages` signature leave the internal `EpochResolver.ingest()` call chain unchanged — confirming the bus-fires-downstream path is not affected — and does `grep -r "subscribeToGroupMessages" app/src/ --include="*.ts" --include="*.tsx" -n` return exactly one call site in `MarmotContext.tsx`?

Does `tsc --noEmit` (or `make build`) succeed with zero TypeScript errors after Story 4 ships — confirming the TypeScript compiler validates that no remaining call site passes `onApplicationMessage` and the type signature change is mechanically complete throughout the codebase?

Do all unit tests that existed before Story 4 (`applicationRumorDispatcher.test.ts`, `chatHandler.test.ts`, `reactionHandler.test.ts`, `profileHandler.test.ts`, `profileRequestHandler.test.ts`, `scoreHandler.test.ts`, `pollHandler.test.ts`) continue to pass under `make test-unit` after the `onApplicationMessage` removal — confirming the mechanical cleanup introduces no regressions in handler unit test coverage?

After removing `onApplicationMessage`, does the internal `EpochResolver` in `welcomeSubscription.ts` still call `mlsGroup.ingest()` on every received rumor — confirming that the removal of the callback parameter does not accidentally disable rumor ingestion into the MLS epoch state machine, which would be a silent data-loss defect?

Does `app/src/lib/marmot/welcomeSubscription.ts`'s `subscribeToGroupMessages` function signature accept only `{ onHistorySynced, onMembersChanged }` in its options object after Story 4 ships — verifiable by (a) `grep "onApplicationMessage" app/src/lib/marmot/welcomeSubscription.ts` returning zero matches, (b) `grep -r "subscribeToGroupMessages" app/src/ --include="*.ts" --include="*.tsx" -n` returning exactly one call site in `MarmotContext.tsx`, and (c) code inspection confirming the call site omits any `onApplicationMessage` argument?

post_impl_questions.question: The no-op `onApplicationMessage: () => {}` passed to EpochResolver inside subscribeToGroupMessages means the EpochResolver's internal callback fires but does nothing. The real application message dispatch path goes through `group.on('applicationMessage', ...)` in applicationRumorDispatcher. Are these two paths guaranteed to both fire for every application message, and does the no-op path in EpochResolver introduce any double-deserialisation cost that should be eliminated by making EpochResolverCallbacks.onApplicationMessage optional?
